macro_segments.title: Introduction and Background: The Three Sources of Jewish Slave Sales in the Torah

micro_segments.title: Overview of the daf and transition from kinyan of woman and yevama to kinyan of eved and ama
The three contexts in which Torah discusses Jewish slave sales: Mishpatim, Devarim, and Behar
Two main categories: mekhuhu Beis Din (sold by court) and mocher atzmo (self-sale), and the bridge of sakhir sakhir

macro_segments.title: The Tanna Kamma's Position: Distinguishing Between Mekhuhu Beis Din and Mocher Atzmo Through Limiting Words

micro_segments.title: Framing the debate: Does sakhir sakhir gezeirah shavah truly bridge the two categories?
The Baraita citing Tanna Kamma and Rabbi Eliezer on differences in halakhot between the two cases

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Position and the Gemara's Challenge: Does Everyone Hold Sakhir Sakhir?

micro_segments.title: Citation of Rav Tavyumi quoting Abaye: fundamentally all agree on gezeirah shavah of sakhir sakhir

macro_segments.title: The Tanna Kamma's Position: Distinguishing Between Mekhuhu Beis Din and Mocher Atzmo Through Limiting Words (continued)

micro_segments.title: First difference: six-year limit only applies to mekhuhu Beis Din, limited by the word 'avadcha'

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Position and the Gemara's Challenge: Does Everyone Hold Sakhir Sakhir? (continued)

micro_segments.title: Reframing the debate: not whether to link the categories, but how to interpret the pesukim's limiting words

macro_segments.title: The Tanna Kamma's Position: Distinguishing Between Mekhuhu Beis Din and Mocher Atzmo Through Limiting Words (continued) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's alternative reading of 'avadcha' as limiting to the heir, not excluding mocher atzmo
Second difference: ear-piercing (retzi'ah) limited by the possessive 'ozno' (his ear)
Debate over whether 'ozno' is a true limiting word or serves another function via gezeirah shavah
Third difference: gifting/laden down with gifts only by mekhuhu Beis Din, limited by 'lo'
Gemara's discussion of whether gifts constitute wages and pass to heirs, with mention of gra's erasure
Alternative interpretation of 'lo': protecting gifts from creditor seizure rather than excluding mocher atzmo
Fourth difference: master can give non-Jewish maidservant only by mekhuhu Beis Din, limited by 'lo'
Discussion of 'mishne sechar sakhir' (double wage) and forced marital relations; alternative interpretation by Tosafot

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Position and the Gemara's Challenge: Does Everyone Hold Sakhir Sakhir? (continued) (continued)

micro_segments.title: Gemara's reframing: perhaps everyone accepts sakhir sakhir; differences are due to limiting words

macro_segments.title: The Opposing View: Rabbi Elazar ben Yaakov and the Question of a Tanna Who Rejects Sakhir Sakhir

micro_segments.title: Citation of braita with Rabbi Elazar ben Yaakov's argument that v'shav el mishpachto implies no sakhir sakhir link
Gemara's counter via Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak: even with sakhir sakhir, extra pasuk needed to address yovel concerns
The two pesukim of v'shavtem and their role in including both mekhuhu Beis Din and nirtza in yovel freedom
Why the extra pesukim are necessary: addressing potential limitations based on the nature of the cases
Reconciliation of the two pesukim: one includes mekhuhu Beis Din, one includes nirtza, both needed
Interpretation of l'olam (forever) and v'shavtem: the role of yovel in limiting eternal servitude

macro_segments.title: Finding a True Dissenter: Rabbi on Self-Sale to Non-Jew and the Limits of Sakhir Sakhir

micro_segments.title: Citation of braita with Rabbi's position: im lo yiga'el ba-eleh; self-sale to non-Jew does not terminate at six years
Rabbi's kal va-chomer: self-sale to non-Jew should differ from self-sale to Jew regarding ge'ulat kerovim
Gemara's counter via Rav Nachman: Rabbi can accept sakhir sakhir but pasuk creates exception
Alternative explanation: yigalenu limits ge'ulat kerovim to non-Jew case, excluding self-sale to Jew

macro_segments.title: Dissenting Views: Rabbi Yossi HaGlili and Rabbi Akiva on Ge'ulat Kerovim vs. Others

micro_segments.title: Braita introducing Rabbi Yossi HaGlili and Rabbi Akiva's dispute over who can redeem from non-Jew and with what consequences
Rabbi Yossi HaGlili: relatives redeem = freedom; others redeem = servitude (working until yovel)
Rabbi Akiva: relatives redeem = servitude; others redeem = freedom (opposite of Yossi)
Interpretation of the three categories (ge'ulat kerovim, ge'ulat atzmo, ge'ulat acherim) in the pasuk
Rabbi Yossi HaGlili's reasoning: mikra nidrash lefanav; link earlier categories to ge'ulat atzmo
Rabbi Akiva's reasoning: mikra nidrash leacharav; link later categories to ge'ulat atzmo
Gemara's point: ba'eleh limits the application; without it both earlier and later would apply to all
Resolution: the debate is ultimately based on svara (reasoning), not purely textual interpretation
Rabbi Yossi HaGlili's svara: others redeeming but remaining enslaved incentivizes them to act; relatives freed ensures their obligation is honored
Rabbi Akiva's svara: relatives freed would cause repeated abuse (selling oneself to relatives); others freed avoids incentivizing self-sale

macro_segments.title: The Consensus View: Rabbi (Judah the Prince) and the Chachamim's Position on Ge'ulat Kerovim

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Chiya bar Abba citing Rabbi Yochanan: the debate between Yossi and Akiva vs. the position of the Chachamim
Chachamim's view: hakol lishichrur (all cases result in freedom, whether relatives or others redeem)
Rabbi's position: he does not use ba'eleh as a limiting word for ge'ulat kerovim; therefore both apply equally
The role of v'yatza b'shnat hayovel: applying to non-Jew under Jewish control, enforcing yovel freedom despite our power

topical_tags.term: Tanna Kamma (first tanna of a dispute)
ahavti et adoni (I love my master)
nirtza (ear-pierced slave who chooses permanent slavery)
v'shav el mishpachto / v'shavtem (return to family/inheritance)
self-sale to non-Jew (mocher atzmo le-oved kochavim)
im lo yiga'el ba-eleh (if not redeemed through these)
v'yatza b'shnat hayovel (goes free in year of jubilee)
